data.title: DEADLINE ALERT: Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP Investigates Claims on Behalf of Investors of Freeport-McMoran
data.description:
DEADLINE ALERT: Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP Investigates Claims on Behalf of Investors of Freeport-McMoran

Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP Securities Litigation Partner James (Josh) Wilson Encourages Investors Who Suffered Losses In Freeport-McMoran To Contact Him Directly To Discuss Their Options

If you purchased or acquired securities in Freeport between February 15, 2022 and September 24, 2025 and would like to discuss your legal rights, call Faruqi & Faruqi partner Josh Wilson directly at 877-247-4292 or 212-983-9330 (Ext. 1310).

[You may also click here for additional information]

Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP, a leading national securities law firm, is investigating potential claims against Freeport-McMoran Inc. (“Freeport” or the “Company”) (NYSE: FCX) and reminds investors of the January 12, 2026 deadline to seek the role of lead plaintiff in a federal securities class action that has been filed against the Company.

Faruqi & Faruqi is a leading national securities law firm with offices in New York, Pennsylvania, California and Georgia. The firm has recovered hundreds of millions of dollars for investors since its founding in 1995. See www.faruqilaw.com.

As detailed below, the complaint alleges that the Company and its executives violated federal securities laws by making false and/or misleading statements and/or failing to disclose that: (1) Freeport did not adequately ensure safety at the Grasberg Block Cave mine in Indonesia; (2) the lack of proper safety precautions constituted a heightened risk that could foreseeably lead to the death of Freeport’s workers; (3) this constituted an undisclosed heightened risk of regulatory, litigation, and reputational risk; and (4) as a result, Defendants’ statements about Freeport-McMoran’s business, operations, and prospects were materially false and misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis at all relevant times.

On September 9, 2025, Freeport disclosed it was suspending mining activities at its Grasberg Block Cave operation in Indonesia, after "a large flow of wet material" trapped seven workers.

On this news, Freeport's stock price fell $2.77, or 5.9%, to close at $43.89 per share on September 9, 2025, thereby injuring investors.

Then, on September 24, 2025, Freeport provided an update on the incident, disclosing that two of the trapped team members "were regrettably fatally injured[.]" Meanwhile, "extensive efforts" remained "ongoing in the search for [the five] team members who [remained] missing."

On this news, Freeport's stock price fell $7.69, or 17%, to close at $37.67 per share on September 24, 2025.

Then, on September 25, 2025, before market hours, Bloomberg published an article stating that the "halt in production at the giant Grasberg copper mine in Indonesia looks set to strain the fractious relationship between [Freeport] and its host nation, at a time when the Jakarta government was already looking to take greater control." The article specified that "[the] state controls 51% of the local entity - after a lengthy battle over ownership - but officials have sporadically continued to demand an increased share. That clamor may now intensify."

On this news, Freeport's stock price fell $2.33, or 6.2%, to close at $35.34 on September 25, 2025, thereby injuring investors further.

On September 28, 2025, a news organization focusing on Indonesia, published an article entitled "Freeport Landslide was Preventable, Not Just a Natural Disaster, Says Expert." The article quoted an expert as saying "this danger is not new and should have been anticipated from the beginning[.]"

The court-appointed lead plaintiff is the investor with the largest financial interest in the relief sought by the class who is adequate and typical of class members who directs and oversees the litigation on behalf of the putative class. Any member of the putative class may move the Court to serve as lead plaintiff through counsel of their choice, or may choose to do nothing and remain an absent class member. Your ability to share in any recovery is not affected by the decision to serve as a lead plaintiff or not.

Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP also encourages anyone with information regarding Freeport’s conduct to contact the firm, including whistleblowers, former employees, shareholders and others.

To learn more about the Freeport-McMoran class action, go to www.faruqilaw.com/FCX or call Faruqi & Faruqi partner Josh Wilson directly at 877-247-4292 or 212-983-9330 (Ext. 1310).

Follow us for updates on LinkedIn, on X, or on Facebook.

Attorney Advertising. The law firm responsible for this advertisement is Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP (www.faruqilaw.com). Prior results do not guarantee or predict a similar outcome with respect to any future matter. We welcome the opportunity to discuss your particular case. All communications will be treated in a confidential manner.

Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP
Josh Wilson
877-247-4292 or 212-983-9330 (Ext. 1310)

data.title: Tech Up Slightly as Volatility Continues — Tech Roundup
data.description:
Shares of technology companies rose slightly as volatility in high-risk corners of the market such as cryptocurrencies and artificial intelligence continued.

Nvidia shares initially led another downdraft in AI companies, and was more than 15% from its recent peak at one stage before recovering some losses.

Tesla shares rose, but remain 7% lower for the year to date.

Germany's SAP proposed changes to how clients can request support and pay for its business-management software, seeking to allay competition concerns from European Union officials weeks after they launched an investigation into the company. 




  Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com 



  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  November 14, 2025 18:05 ET (23:05 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Consumer Cos Slip Amid Holiday Shopping Concerns — Consumer Roundup
data.description:
Consumer companies slipped, compounding losses from earlier in the week, amid concerns about the key holiday shopping season.

Economists say the consumer sector has seen a bifurcation, with luxury brands catering to wealthy consumers thriving even as middle-class providers, such as Target, struggle.

Swiss luxury firm Richemont, the owner of upscale brands including jeweler Cartier, reported a pickup in demand with fiscal second-quarter sales rising a robust 14%.

In an acknowledgment of middle-class struggles making ends meet, the Trump administration said there were plans to roll back tariffs on key grocery items.

Walmart Chief Executive Doug McMillon stepped down from his position after more than 15 years with the company, to be succeeded by President John Furner.

Shares of driving range chain Topgolf Callaway rose after reports that private-equity firm Leonard Green was in talks to buy the chain out for about $1 billion. 




  Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com 



  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  November 14, 2025 17:50 ET (22:50 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Elon Musk now says xAI is delaying the Grok 5 launch till next year
data.description:
By William Gavin

Musk has said the AI chatbot will 'feel sentient,' and there's a chance it will achieve human-level intelligence

Elon Musk's founded his artificial intelligence startup in spring 2023. Its primary product is Grok, a large language model.

Elon Musk said Friday that the next version of his artificial intelligence chatbot will blow all of its rivals out of the water - after some extra training time.

"Grok 5, I think, will be the smartest AI in the world by a significant margin in every metric - without exception," the xAI founder said Friday in an appearance at Baron Capital's annual investment conference.

Musk had said in August that he planned to debut what he called the "crushingly good" next version of Grok by the end of 2025, but said Friday that it will instead come out within the first three months of next year. Grok 5 will launch with twice as many parameters - the settings that influence the behavior of large language models - as the current model, he said.

The new model will be both "extremely" intelligent and fast, Musk said, noting that he thinks there is a roughly 10% chance for the chatbot to achieve human-level intelligence. He added that xAI has been training Grok to be better with using tools, like searching the internet, and understanding live videos.

"I've never thought that before," Musk said of the possibility Grok could achieve what experts call artificial general intelligence. "It's really gonna feel sentient," he added.

Grok has primarily been used alongside Musk's companies, most prominently in X, which xAI acquired in March. The chatbot has also been implemented in Tesla's (TSLA) electric vehicles and powers customer-support features for SpaceX's satellite internet service. Musk also wants Grok to be embedded in Tesla's humanoid robots.

Scaling AI development comes down to a few things, including attracting the right talent and installing a lot of hardware as quickly as possible, according to Musk. As the industry has found out, that can get very expensive.

xAI's Colossus data center in Memphis, Tennessee, has at least 200,000 graphic processing units already installed, but the company plans to eventually use one million units in the facility. The startup has been burning through about $1 billion each month as it spends heavily on developing its AI models, Bloomberg reported in June.

See More: Big Tech needs a staggering $1.5 trillion to fund the AI boom. This is the complex playbook it's using to get it.

Meta Platforms (META) alone plans to have as much as $72 billion in capital expenditures this year, with most of that cash going toward AI infrastructure. That's expected to continue in 2026, although the tech giant also expects to spend a large chunk of change to compensate employees.

Earlier this year, Meta offered up pay packages worth as much as $300 million to poach top-level researchers from rival OpenAI, according to Wired. In June, Meta invested $14 billion in Scale AI and hired CEO Alexandr Wang to lead its superintelligence lab. The company cut some 600 positions in its AI unit last month, Axios reported.

Alphabet (GOOGL) expects capex of between $91 billion and $93 billion in 2025, while Amazon.com (AMZN) plans to spend $125 billion and be "very aggressive" in building data centers. Microsoft, which has partnered with OpenAI, had $34.9 billion in capex in its fiscal first quarter, $10 billion more than analysts had expected.

-William Gavin

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  11-14-25 1751ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: I have a $10K expense. Do I withdraw money from my investments or my high-yield savings account?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'It will likely take me about a year to replace the $10,000'

"It will likely take me about a year to replace the $10,000." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

I need to pay for an expense of about $10,000. I have the money available, both in a high-yield savings account (short-term return of 3.5%) and in investments (index fund with a long-term return of 7%). It will likely take me about a year to replace the $10,000. If you were me, where would you recommend I withdraw it from?

Caught Between a Rock and ...

Related: 'I'm in a financial mess': My income was cut in half. Do I sell my $600K home and kiss my 2.9% mortgage rate goodbye?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Take it from your high-yield savings account. That's the path of least resistance.

Dear Rock,

You're actually caught between a rock and an easy place.

Take it from your high-yield savings account, and replenish it over time. That's the path of least resistance - and expenses like these are the reason to have liquid savings that you can access in an emergency. The math also backs up that choice: If you took $10,000 out of your high-yield savings, over a year you'd lose $350 in interest. If you withdrew that money from your index fund, at a 7% annual return, you could lose $750 over 12 months (depending, of course, on what happens with the market).

For context, the S&P 500 SPX is up more than 15% year to date, while the Nasdaq Composite COMP is up nearly 20%. The Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA has increased by roughly 13%, so had you taken this $10,000 at the beginning of the year, you would have lost more than that $750. The opposite is also true: If you had taken the money when the market was at an all-time high but it fell later in the year, you might not regret it. Still, trying to time the market is a fool's game.

Share prices rise and fall based on fundamentals, and the tech sector has been making money hand over fist, although investors are increasingly concerned about the billions of dollars being spent on artificial intelligence and wondering if that spending will pay off. But markets also move in accordance with expectations, which can change like the weather. There are persistent concerns about high valuations, and some economists are predicting a correction.

You're fortunate that you have an emergency fund. Even those people who follow the advice to leave their investments untouched may not have another option when faced with an unexpected expense. "Although money in retirement accounts is intended to be preserved for retirement, occasionally these savings can also act as a source of emergency funds for non-retirees who face economic hardships," according to this report released by the Federal Reserve last year.

"Reducing regular contributions to retirement accounts is another way that non-retirees can increase their disposable income when facing economic challenges," the Fed said. Some 8% of nonretired adults tapped their retirement savings by borrowing from or cashing out funds from their retirement accounts in the prior 12 months, while another 8% of nonretirees said that they had reduced their regular contributions to their retirement accounts in the previous 12 months.

Nor are you the only person who feels caught between a rock and a hard place. This Moneyist reader incurred a $12,000 credit-card debt due to necessary repairs on their car and home, health issues and a 30% cut in their annual pay. As I told them, not everyone has the capacity to build an emergency fund that would cover expenses for 12 or even six months, especially with the rise in the cost of living; high, if slowly falling, interest rates; and growing uncertainty in the job market. But you do have the cash.

The long-term outlook for you is rosier than for many people. You are one of the 62% of Americans who are invested in the stock market, at least according to this Gallup poll. "Among households earning $100,000 or more a year, about 87% own stock," according to the survey. "But when household income drops to $50,000, the ownership rate goes down to just 28%." On that basis, it's probable that you have a healthy income that can help you replenish your savings.

If you built up these savings once, hopefully you can do it again.

Don't miss: 'I fear a significant decline in the S&P 500': Do I sell my tech stocks before it's too late?

Check out the Moneyist private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

My siblings don't earn nearly as much as me. Should my parents leave me less money in their will?

I'm 83 and will have $500K if I sell my house. My estranged wife wants me to join her in Colombia. Do I go?

I'm 53, a single parent with a $1 million home. My boyfriend wants to marry. Am I better off staying single?

By emailing your questions to the Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on the Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  11-14-25 1739ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Health Care Down, But Not by Much, as Deal Activity Continues — Health Care Roundup
data.description:
Health-care companies fell, but not by as much as the broad market, as deal activity in the sector continued.

Merck struck a $9.2 billion deal to buy biotechnology company Cidara Therapeutics in a move that bolsters the drugmaker's respiratory portfolio and pipeline as it braces for the looming loss of patent exclusivity for the blockbuster cancer drug Keytruda.

Closely held Purdue Pharma won bankruptcy court approval for an opioid-related settlement of at least $7.4 billion, clearing a path for the OxyContin maker to exit its six-year bankruptcy and resolve mass lawsuits by governments, health-care providers and individuals. 




  Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com 



  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  November 14, 2025 17:41 ET (22:41 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Materials Down as Dollar Gains — Materials Roundup
data.description:
Producers of metals and other raw materials fell as the U.S. dollar gained in light of mounting doubts about the Federal Reserve's December policy statement.

The Fed Funds futures markets now indicate that the decision will be a toss-up between another rate cut and a pause, with increasingly vocal proponents of each action. Strategists at one brokerage said the Fed's minutes will likely provide further evidence of this split.

On Thursday, a Trump administration official told reporters that the White House plans to eliminate tariffs on coffee, beef, bananas and some textiles and apparel from four Latin American countries: Argentina, Ecuador, El Salvador and Guatemala.

Brazilian meatpacker JBS, the largest beef processor in the U.S., is leaning into the pricier Wagyu and expanding ground-beef production to help cope with rising cattle costs, as the U.S. cattle herd shrinks to its smallest size since the 1950s.

Gold futures and silver futures fell significantly for the session due to interest-rate concerns, but remained higher for the week following a record close for silver Wednesday.

BHP Group intends to appeal a decision by the English High Court that said it is liable for the deadly collapse of the Fundao dam in Brazil 10 years ago. 




  Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com 



  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  November 14, 2025 17:33 ET (22:33 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
